title: yrPublish Documentation
text:
1 index.qmd
Welcome to the yrPublish documentation. This book gathers the project’s specs, setup instructions, and reference material in one place. This documentation all lives in the docs folder of the yrPublish repo. The documentation is maintained as a Quarto book. To render the documentation, naviate to the docs folder and run quarto preview.
How to use this book
Below is the contents of the README.md that gets displayed on github.com Other files in this book have names that should clearly indicate what they contain.

title: yrPublish Documentation
text:
2.1 What is Quarto?
Quarto is a publishing system that can render entire projects into structured outputs such as:

Websites
Books
Manuscripts

It allows for output formats of HTML, PDF, Word and other formats.

title: yrPublish Documentation
text:
2.2 What does this repo add?
This repo attempts to address the following limitations of Quarto (as of today, March 20, 2026). There may be additional features added in the future.

Quarto books cannot have custom chapter numbers. Chapter numbers always start from 1 and are sequential. This repo adds the ability to have custom chapter numbers.
Quarto books have “chapters”. Large books with many chapters can segment the chapters of a book among different “part”s of the book. However, there is no way to have nested parts for books that become much larger.
One of the goals of this project is to allow for a much deeper nesting of a hierarchical organization scheme for very large “books” or collections of material.

title: yrPublish Documentation
text:
2.3 More about yrPublish
In general yrPublish adds several assets and imposes some constraints on how to create publishable content. There are some Bash scripts, custom css and javascript etc that are used with this system.
yrPublish is currently envisioned as a CLI tool with a single command, “yrPublish” with possibly numerous “sub commands”.
The general workflow to render a book built with this system is:

Create the repo using the yrPublish create-project my-project to create a new repo. This is similar to the similar quarto command. This command creates the folder structure and adds any files or assets that are needed for a new project.
Content creators add content in the same general way that they would for quarto files. This includes editing .qmd files, creating a _quarto.yml file, etc. However, content editors that are using the yrPublish system should make sure to adhere to the yrPublish guidelines. For example, if a content creator wants custom chapter numbers for their book they should follow the directions in the docs for yrPublish to do so. Currently this includes

editing the _quarto.yml file with special directives to record the custom chapter numbers
running some yrPublish specific scripts prior to rendering the project with the quarto cli tools. The entire process is documented in the yrPublish docs folder.

title: yrPublish Documentation
text:
2.4 Documentation for yrPublish
The docs folder in this repo contains a quarto book with the documentation for this system. It also includes any specs for updates to the yrPublish system itself.
AI systems that are editing this repo should make sure to keep the documents in the docs folder as well as this README.md file up to date with all changes that they make.

title: yrPublish Documentation
section: 2.5 Goal is to be a quarto compliant as possible
text:
2.5 Goal is to be a quarto compliant as possible
Note that the Quarto system is under ongoing development and may add features in the future that yrPublish had already added.
If/when that happens the goal of this system is to prefer yrPublish projects to use the native Quarto features as much as possible. The goal of yrPublish is NOT to reinvent the wheel (at least not yet) but to add features to Quarto where they are missing.

title: 2  DOC-GUIDE-v001.qmd
text:
2.1 Reading the docs as an HTML book
This document describes the various types of documentation files and the version numbering scheme.
The documentation in docs/ can be rendered as a single Quarto book (HTML) for easier reading. To build or rebuild the book after documentation changes, see QUARTO-DOC-GUIDE.qmd. That guide explains how to install the Quarto tools (if needed) and how to regenerate the book. The built book is in docs/_book/; open docs/_book/index.html in a browser to read it.
In the book, the sidebar (table of contents) lists each chapter, and the page title is usually the same as the document’s heading (H1) or filename. To keep the TOC stable in this repo, follow these conventions for chapters listed in docs/_quarto.yml:
Exception: the chapter that displays the repository README is built from Overview.qmd, which only includes README.md. In the sidebar it appears as README; on the webpage, the top-level heading is taken directly from README.md (e.g. “yrS3 — Tutorial & Testing System”), not from the Overview filename. Edit only README.md to change that content; the book updates automatically when rebuilt.

title: 2  DOC-GUIDE-v001.qmd
text:
For normal docs under docs/, set the single level-1 heading (# ...) to the exact .qmd filename (including the .qmd extension).
For llmChats/**/prompt.qmd and llmChats/**/prompt.md, set the single level-1 heading (# ...) to the containing folder name (the directory that holds the prompt.* file).

title: 2  DOC-GUIDE-v001.qmd
text:
2.2 v001 version format
In general, any document (including this one) can have a -v001 format style version number at the end of its name. This notation is used to ensure that it is easy to compare different versions of the same document - without needing to know how to use GIT commands.

title: 2  DOC-GUIDE-v001.qmd
text:
2.3 SPEC documents
This system will be continuously developed in an incremental way. Each incremental development phase will be versioned in 0.0.1 style. Each incremental development phase will have a spec document named SPEC-v0.0.1.md or something similar, where the version number for the spec matches the version number of the release.

title: 2  DOC-GUIDE-v001.qmd
text:
2.4 PRD documents
PRD stands for Product Requirements Document. In this project, a PRD is a living document that describes the goals and vision for the full system—closer to a product vision than to a minimal “requirements list.” The project relies heavily on LLM-assisted coding, and PRD is a term that LLMs recognize and understand, so the files are named PRD (e.g. PRD-0.0.1-v001.qmd) and this guide uses that term for consistency.
The names of PRD documents e.g. PRD-0.0.1-v001 represent (a) the SPEC version that was active at the time the PRD was modified, as well as a 2ndary ver number. The ver number at the end of the name of a PRD document represents different versions of the PRD for a particular incremental development step. If the PRD was not modified at a particular incremental development step, then no new PRD version would be created at that step and the PRD version number will remain unchanged for that step. Therefore the PRD version numbers could skip numbers, for example if the PRD was modified when SPEC-v0.0.1 was created and then again when SPEC-v0.0.5 was created then the only versions of the PRD would be PRD-v0.0.1 and PRD-v0.0.5.

title: 3  DOC-AUTHORING-GUIDE.qmd
text:
4 DOC-AUTHORING-GUIDE.qmd
This guide explains how to write and maintain documentation for this system. Follow these conventions so the docs stay consistent, render correctly as a Quarto book, and remain easy to navigate and update.

title: 3  DOC-AUTHORING-GUIDE.qmd
text:
4.1 Document format: use Quarto (.qmd)

Write all documentation in Quarto format using the .qmd extension.
.qmd files support standard Markdown plus Quarto features (cross-references, code execution, conditional content, and a single YAML header for metadata).
Use a YAML header at the top of each document with at least a title:
---
title: "Your document title"
---
To include a doc in the rendered book, add it to docs/_quarto.yml in the desired order. See QUARTO-DOC-GUIDE.qmd for how to build the book.

title: 3  DOC-AUTHORING-GUIDE.qmd
text:
4.2 Version numbering
Follow the version numbering scheme described in DOC-GUIDE-v001.qmd:

Use the -v001-style suffix in document names when you want to track versions (e.g. SETUP-0.0.1-v001.qmd, DOC-GUIDE-v001.qmd).
SPEC documents are versioned to match releases (e.g. SPEC-0.0.1).
PRD and other living docs can carry both a SPEC version and a secondary version in the filename when they are updated.

Do not encode version numbers in the body of the document (e.g. avoid “see Section 2.3”); use cross-references instead so numbers stay correct when sections change.

title: 3  DOC-AUTHORING-GUIDE.qmd
text:
4.3 README and the book
The repository README is README.md in the project root (for GitHub). It is the single source of truth for the project overview. The book includes it via docs/Overview.qmd; edit only the root README so the repo and the book stay in sync. The Overview chapter is an exception: it has no level-1 heading in the .qmd file; the page title and single level-1 heading come only from the included README.md (e.g. # yrS3 — Tutorial & Testing System). The sidebar shows “README” (from the YAML title in Overview.qmd) so readers know the chapter is the repo README.

title: 3  DOC-AUTHORING-GUIDE.qmd
section: 4.4 One top-level heading per document (Quarto book standard)
text:
4.4 One top-level heading per document (Quarto book standard)

Each document should have exactly one level-1 heading (#). That heading is the document’s main title (or the main title is set in the YAML title and the single # is the same or a short form).
All other sections should use ## (level 2), ### (level 3), and so on. Do not add a second # in the same file.
This matches Quarto book authoring practice: the first heading level defines the document’s top-level structure, and having a single # keeps numbering and navigation consistent when the book is built.


4.4.1 TOC/title labeling convention for this repo
To ensure that the HTML book sidebar label and the page’s top-level heading match the identifiers you expect, use this rule:

For normal docs listed in the book (e.g. docs/*.qmd chapters), set the single level-1 heading to the exact .qmd filename (including .qmd).
For docs/llmChats/**/prompt.qmd and docs/llmChats/**/prompt.md, set the single level-1 heading to the containing folder name (the directory that holds the prompt.* file).

title: 3  DOC-AUTHORING-GUIDE.qmd
section: 4.5 Do not use hardcoded section numbers
text:
4.5 Do not use hardcoded section numbers

Do not put section numbers in headings (e.g. avoid ## 2. Local development or ### 1.1 Node.js). Quarto will number sections automatically when the book is rendered.
Do not refer to sections by hand-typed numbers in the text (e.g. “see Section 2.3” or “as in Section 1.6”). Those numbers go out of date when you add, remove, or reorder sections.

title: 3  DOC-AUTHORING-GUIDE.qmd
text:
4.6 Use Quarto cross-references for sections

Give each section that you might want to reference a label (ID) in the heading using the {#sec-id} syntax:
## Local development {#sec-local-development}
### Supabase project setup (CLI-first where possible) {#sec-supabase-project-setup}
Use short, stable, descriptive IDs (e.g. #sec-supabase-cli, #sec-configure-tests). Avoid spaces; use hyphens. The sec- prefix is the Quarto convention for section labels.
In the text, refer to that section with @sec-id. Quarto will replace it with the correct section number and, in HTML, a clickable link:
For a hosted project, see @sec-supabase-project-setup.
If you run Supabase locally, follow @sec-running-supabase-locally.
Rendered output will show the correct number (e.g. “Section 2.3”) and link to the section. When you add or reorder sections, only the labels need to stay the same; the numbers update automatically.

title: 3  DOC-AUTHORING-GUIDE.qmd
text:
4.7 Other Quarto book authoring practices

Links to other docs: Prefer relative links to other .qmd files in the repo (e.g. [DOC-GUIDE-v001.qmd](DOC-GUIDE-v001.qmd)) so links work in both the source and the built book.
Code blocks: Use fenced code blocks with a language identifier (e.g. ```bash, ```toml) for syntax highlighting.
Consistency: Use the same heading hierarchy and naming style across documents so the book table of contents and cross-references stay predictable.
Building the book: After changing docs, rebuild with quarto render from the docs/ directory (or as described in QUARTO-DOC-GUIDE.qmd) and check that cross-references and numbering look correct in docs/_book/.

title: 3  DOC-AUTHORING-GUIDE.qmd
text:
4.8 Summary checklist
When writing or editing a doc:

Use .qmd with a YAML title that matches the document’s single level-1 heading (H1). (If you omit YAML, still ensure the single H1 matches what you want in the TOC.)
Follow the version numbering in DOC-GUIDE-v001.qmd for filenames.
Use exactly one # (top-level) heading per document; use ##, ###, etc. for subsections.
Do not hardcode section numbers in headings or in the text.
Add {#sec-id} to headings you might reference; use @sec-id in the text to refer to them.

title: 4  QUARTO-DOC-GUIDE.qmd
text:
4.1 What gets built
This document explains how to build and regenerate the HTML documentation book for this project. The book is built with Quarto and turns the markdown files in docs/ into a single navigable HTML site.

title: 4  QUARTO-DOC-GUIDE.qmd
text:
Source: All .qmd files in docs/ that are listed in docs/_quarto.yml.
Output: Rendered HTML and assets go into docs/_book/. Open docs/_book/index.html in a browser to read the book.

title: 4  QUARTO-DOC-GUIDE.qmd
text:
4.2 When to regenerate
Regenerate the book whenever you:

Add, remove, or rename documentation files (and update _quarto.yml if needed).
Edit any of the docs that are included in the book.

title: 4  QUARTO-DOC-GUIDE.qmd
text:
4.3 Installing Quarto
You need the Quarto CLI to render the book. If quarto --version works in your terminal, you can skip this section.

4.3.1 Option A: Official installer (recommended)

Go to https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/.
Choose your OS and follow the install steps. On Linux you can use the .deb package or the tarball; on macOS, the .pkg; on Windows, the .msi.



4.3.2 Option B: Linux (tarball, single user)
Replace VERSION with a release from Quarto releases (e.g. 1.4.553):
# Download and extract
wget https://github.com/quarto-dev/quarto-cli/releases/download/v${VERSION}/quarto-${VERSION}-linux-amd64.tar.gz
mkdir -p ~/opt
tar -C ~/opt -xzf quarto-${VERSION}-linux-amd64.tar.gz

# Make quarto available (adjust if you use a different bin directory)
mkdir -p ~/.local/bin
ln -sf ~/opt/quarto-${VERSION}/bin/quarto ~/.local/bin/quarto

# Ensure ~/.local/bin is in PATH (e.g. add to ~/.bashrc or ~/.profile)
export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"


4.3.3 Verify
quarto check
This checks the CLI and suggests any missing tools (e.g. Pandoc).

title: 4  QUARTO-DOC-GUIDE.qmd
text:
4.4 Regenerating the book
From inside the docs/ directory (recommended):
cd docs
quarto render
Or from the project root (the directory that contains docs/):
quarto render docs
If you see errors about missing environment variables when running from the project root, run the command from inside docs/ instead so only the book project is used.
After a successful run, open docs/_book/index.html in your browser to view the book.

4.4.1 Optional: preview while editing
To auto-rebuild when files change and optionally open the book in a browser:
quarto preview docs
Press Ctrl+C to stop the preview server.

title: 4  QUARTO-DOC-GUIDE.qmd
text:
4.5 Adding or removing chapters
The book’s table of contents (TOC) on the left is defined in docs/_quarto.yml under book.chapters. Use the steps below so that TOC labels (and page H1 headings) match the conventions used in this repo, and when you have multiple versions of a doc, the most recent appears first.

4.5.1 How to add a new doc

Add the .qmd file (e.g. My-New-Doc.qmd) to docs/.
Set the document’s single level-1 heading (# ...) to the identifier you want in the TOC:

For normal docs (not in llmChats/**), use the exact .qmd filename (including .qmd).
For llmChats/**/prompt.qmd and llmChats/**/prompt.md, use the containing folder name (the directory that holds prompt.*).

If you use a YAML header, set title to the same string as the H1 so everything stays consistent:
---
title: "My-New-Doc.qmd"  # must match the H1 exactly
---
In docs/_quarto.yml, add the file under the right part in book.chapters. Order matters: list items in the order you want them in the TOC:
chapters:
  - part: "Reference"
    chapters:
      - QUESTION-FORMAT-0.0.1.qmd
      - SETUP-0.0.1-v001.qmd
      - My-New-Doc.qmd   # new file
When there are multiple versions of the same document (e.g. PRD-0.0.1-v003.qmd, PRD-0.0.1-v002.qmd, PRD-0.0.1-v001.qmd), list them with the most recent version at the top in _quarto.yml. So the newest PRD comes first, then older ones:
- part: "PRD"
  chapters:
    - PRD-0.0.1-v003.qmd   # most recent first
    - PRD-0.0.1-v002.qmd
    - PRD-0.0.1-v001.qmd
Run quarto render docs (or quarto render from inside docs/) again.

To remove a chapter, delete or comment out its entry in _quarto.yml and re-render.

title: 4  QUARTO-DOC-GUIDE.qmd
text:
4.6 Summary



Task
Command (from project root)




Build the book
quarto render docs


Preview live
quarto preview docs


View the book
Open docs/_book/index.html in a browser



For more on Quarto books, see Quarto — Creating a Book.

title: 5  BackgroundAndFeatures-0.0.1-v001.qmd
text:
5.1 Background
The origins of the yrPublish system grew out of my working with the Quarto system and creating some customizations for myself. In this document I try to capture the cusomizations that I created and used across several different quaro projects that I wrote, maintain and continue to develop.
I have been creating several Quarto “book” projects that I render to HTML. Most of these are to support the classes that I teach. Some of these “books” have gotten quite large. I add material pretty regularly and these books gorw in size dynamically as time goes on. I keep the source code checked into git/github.
For most of my books I have a github repo whose name ends in “Src”, e.g. myBookSrc. I have another github repo with the same base name, but that does NOT have the Src suffix, eg myBook. The Src repo contains the source code for the repo (mostly Quarto .qmd files). When I’m ready to publish the next version of the book to the Internet, I render the Src repo with Quarto. I then copy the HTMl/css/javascript and other rendered assets to my repo that does not have the Src extension.
So far, I have been hosting the rendered books in Github and serving them via github Pages. Sometimes I have more than one version of the book that I host at different github Pages sites. For example
Of couse the rendered versions of the books can be hosted anywhere, not just github Pages. However, github Pages is convenient for me for now.

title: 5  BackgroundAndFeatures-0.0.1-v001.qmd
text:
rnotesSrc is a repo that contains the source code for a book I wrote about R.
rnotes is a repo that contains just the rendered version of the book. I host access to this book via github pages at https://y-rosenthal.github.io/rnotes
rnotesLatest is another github repo where I host the bleeding edge version of the rendered rnotesSrc book.

title: 5  BackgroundAndFeatures-0.0.1-v001.qmd
text:
5.2 AI assisted development
I am using AI to help me develop the yrPublish product. Therefore I have structured this repo in a way that I think best aids AI in helping me.

5.2.1 docs folder
All documentation for the system, including user guides, as well as development documentation such as SPEC files are located in the docs folder. The docs are generally Quarto .qmd files and the entire docs folder is rendered as a “plain” Quarto book (i.e. not a yrPublish rendered book).


5.2.2 sampleBooks folder
While developing this repo I thought it would be helpful for the AI to have access to the actual code from some of the book repos that I have been developing. I started with just one, so as not to overwhelm the AI. This one book is located in sampleBooks/DataManagementUsingRSrc. This has the .git folder for the repo in it also so that if desired, the AI can review the git history for that repo.

title: 5  BackgroundAndFeatures-0.0.1-v001.qmd
section: 5.3 For large books I currently use the Quarto “part” concept
text:
5.3 For large books I currently use the Quarto “part” concept
For larger books, I use the quarto “part” concept to segment the book for easier navigation. For example these are some books that I created: https://y-rosenthal.github.io/DataManagementUsingR/ https://y-rosenthal.github.io/excelNotes/ https://y-rosenthal.github.io/rnotesLatest/ (see screen shots)

5.3.1 -v00n version numbers
On many of my files, I include a “-v00N” version number that I increment when I create a new version of the file. I started using this system before I started to use git. However, even though I started to use git I find that it is still helpful sometimes to have a version number directly in the name of a file. In that way, if I use the same file in different books, I can very easily see which version of the file I’m using. For example, I am currently up to v004 for the yrChapterNumber Bash script. Therefore, the actual name of the script that I would run would be “yrChapterNumber-v004.sh”. For Bash scripts and utilities, I generally only increment the -v00N number when I make breaking changes. So I might checking numerous non-breaking changes to yrChapterNumber-v004 (e.g. changing the comments).
For .qmd files I have a bunch of -v00N files from before I started using git. Now that I am using git, I just kept the same filenames. Sometimes, I might create a new file with a -v00N extension even though I’m using git. The motivation is often that I want more than one versin of a file to be immediately available in the repo for convenience purposes.
This may not be the best system but it works for me now. It’s a system with very loose rules. I just want you to know what the -v00N endings on many files are in my repos.

title: 5  BackgroundAndFeatures-0.0.1-v001.qmd
section: 5.4 Custom chapter numbers (enabled via css and javascript at runtime)
text:
5.4 Custom chapter numbers (enabled via css and javascript at runtime)

5.4.1 motivation
I often modify the books in the middle of my teaching a course. Sometimes, I might add a chapter in the middle of the book which by default would cause the later chapter numbers to be incremented. I didn’t like that since I didn’t want to change the chapter numbers in the book in the middle of a course after students had already become familiar with the book’s layout. My thought was to label a new chapter that appears after chapter 12 for example, not as chapter 13, but rather as chapter 12b. In that way I could keep all existing chapter numbers. Unfortunately, Quarto doesn’t seem to have a standard way to have custom chapter names. Therefore I devised my own solution using custom markup and Javascript.


5.4.2 custom markup
I added a  element to every first level heading in every page in the book. I then also added custom comments to the _quarto.yml at the end of every chapter line in the yml file that shows the preferred chapter number. For example, the following might be in a _quarto.yml. It shows the chapter numbers I want after # yrChapterNumber. In the following example (there is no actual book with the contents of this example), I might have had a book with 5 chapters (that didn’t have the emptyTags.qmd chapter). Suppose, I would want to add the emptyTags.qmd chapter in the middle of the semester. I would want the chapter number to be 1b and keep the rest of the chapter numbers the same.
- part: Intro to HTML
  chapters:
  - introToElements.qmd      # yrChapterNumber 1
  - emptyTags.qmd            # yrChapterNumber 1b
  - pageStructure.qmd        # yrChapterNumber 2
  - tableTags.qmd           # yrChapterNumber  3

- part: Intro to CSS
  chapters:
  - styleTag.qmd            # yrChapterNumber  4
  - basicSelectors.qmd      # yrChapterNumber  5
To support this, all .qmd files would have single top level # header at the top of the file. The text of the # header in a .qmd file becomes the text displayed in the table of contents (TOC) on the left of the Quarto book website. To support custom chapter numbers I added a  element in the single top level # heading in all .qmd files in the Quarto book. I also created a Bash script, yrChapterNumber.sh, that gets the preferred chapter numbers from the _quarto.yml and populates the  elements with the correct chapter numbers. I would run this bash script prior to rendering the book. So for exmple after running the yrChapterNumber.sh script I might have the following:
The first line of the “introToElements.qmd” file might ha: # 1.  Introduction to HTML element
The first line of the “emptyTags.qmd” might be: # 1b.  Empty tableTags
The first line of the pageStructure.qmd file might be: # 2.  Structure of a valid HTML page
etc.


5.4.3 Bash scripts
I have the following bash scripts:

yrChapterNumber.sh - described above in the previous section
yrRenumberChapter.sh :
Suppose for the next version of the book I want to start again with sequential chapter numbers. I would run the command yrRenumberChapters _quarto.yml. The script with change the numbers in the #yrChapterNumber comments in the _quarto.yml to be sequestial numbers, e.g. 1,2,3,4,5,6 instead of 1,1b,2,3,4,5 that was there before.
yrBuildQuarto.sh
I have a custom build script to support any other custom processing that I need to do to render the Quarto. For example at some point I think I needed to have the _quarto.yml file in the _book folder that contains the rendered website. The _quarto.yml is processed at runtime by custom javascript (in file yrstuff-v001.js)

I believe that




5.4.4 suppress original chapter numbers with custom css
The original Quarto rendered chapter numbers are suppressed from being displayed with the custom css in the file yrStyles-main-v0001.css, which currently contains the following:
.chapter-number {
    display: none;
}

yrchapternumber { font-size: 99%; color: red; }

.yrAppendixChapterNumber {
    display: none;
}


5.4.5 Custom javascript
I have custom javascript that renumbers the chapters from the numbers that Quarto renders into the book, into the the numbers that are in the  elements. (Note that appendices are treated a little differently due to how quarto renders appendix numbers and names).
I’ll tell you the truth - I don’t remember exactly how the following comes into play in the custom chapter numbering system system. However, it all works and I’m afraid to change it. Perhaps you could help me remember and understand how I designed this or perhaps you could point out some things that could be improved?
The javascript code in question in the file yrstuff-v001.js I believe it is part of this system. It parses the _quarto.yml file to map the new chapter numbers to the names of the qmd/html files. For this reason the _quarto.yml file must be in the rendered website’s folder (i.e. it must be copied to the _book folder at render time). This is accomplished by the R program, copy_quartoYml_to_bookFolder-v001.R program that is referenced in the pre-render: section in _quarto.yml. Note that the _quarto.yml file is also in the resources: section of the _quarto.yml file - I’m not sure if that is truly necessary or not - I don’t recall.
In the post-render: section of the _quarto.yml, I am also running an R script named yrZipQmdFiles.R that creates a .zip file from each .qmd file and puts it in the _book directory. I’m not 100% sure why I did that. If you can figure it out, let me know … I think that I might have wanted to add a link to each rendered HTML page that allows for the download of the source for the page. However, I don’t think I actually implemented that part - again I’m not sure - help me figure this out please …

title: 5  BackgroundAndFeatures-0.0.1-v001.qmd
text:
5.5 Open questions
I have a few questions. I hope the AI can help me figure this out.
QUESTIONS:
The following are the first few lines of my _quarto.yml file
project:
  type: book
  output-dir: _book
  pre-render: copy_quartoYml_to_bookFolder-v001.R
  post-render: Rscript yrZipQmdFiles.R
  resources:
    - _quarto.yml
    - yrNocacheHeaders-v001.html
    - yrstuff-v001.js
    - assignment-api-0000100-weatherDataApi-v002.qmd.zip
    - assignment-api-0000100-weatherDataApi-v001.qmd.zip
  render:
    - "*.qmd"  # Ensures all .qmd files are rendered, even if not in `chapters`
    - "assignment-api-0000100-weatherDataApi-v001.qmd"  # Ensure the old version gets rendered

why did I use the Rscript command only in the post-render section but not in the pre-render section?
Is there any reference to or use of the .zip files that I create in the post-render step in the finished rendered website?
Why do I include _quarto.yml in the resources: section and also copy the _quarto.yml file to the _book folder in the pre-render: section
I’m not sure why I included the following. I think it was something I did when I was in a rush to get something working. If you see any particular reason why this is necessary, let me know. I don’t think this is necessary for the general approach to building these types of books.
render:
  - "*.qmd"  # Ensures all .qmd files are rendered, even if not in `chapters`
  - "assignment-api-0000100-weatherDataApi-v001.qmd"  # Ensure the old version gets rendered

title: 5  BackgroundAndFeatures-0.0.1-v001.qmd
text:
5.6 Cursor AI answers (from Cursor’s AI)

5.6.1 Why did I use Rscript in post-render but not in pre-render?
Cursor AI answer: Quarto’s project pre-render / post-render scripts are supported for multiple languages (including R) via Quarto’s project-script mechanism. So the “correctness” generally does not depend on whether the entry is written as bare somefile.R or as an explicit Rscript somefile.R invocation. It’s most likely historical/style/convenience: pre-render: copy_quartoYml_to_bookFolder-v001.R is already valid, and post-render: Rscript yrZipQmdFiles.R is effectively just making the interpreter explicit.


5.6.2 Is there any reference to or use of the .zip files created in post-render?
Cursor AI answer: In your described setup, the .zip files are created into _book and are listed under resources: in _quarto.yml, which means they are intended to be published as static assets. However, that does not automatically mean the existing site UI/JS references them. In most implementations of this pattern, the zips are used for “downloadable source” (or archival) even if you haven’t yet added a link/button per page. To confirm actual usage, you’d look for references to those zip filenames in templates/HTML/JS; based on the repo structure here, the published assets exist, but the linkage may be unimplemented.


5.6.3 Why include _quarto.yml in resources: and also copy _quarto.yml to _book in pre-render?
Cursor AI answer: resources: is a Quarto mechanism for shipping static files into the output directory, so including _quarto.yml there ensures it becomes available in the built output. Separately, your pre-render step copies _quarto.yml into _book so runtime client-side JS (like yrstuff-v001.js) can fetch _quarto.yml from the final site. Keeping both is likely redundant: one mechanism may have been added to guarantee a working behavior during experimentation, or to ensure timing/paths matched what the JS fetch expects. Either way, the functional goal is the same: make _quarto.yml reliably available under _book/ at runtime.


5.6.4 Why include render: - "*.qmd" and also explicitly list one older .qmd?
Cursor AI answer: For type: book, the chapters: list controls what Quarto includes in navigation, but Quarto may still skip rendering some files depending on project configuration and incremental rendering. render: "*.qmd" is a way to force rendering of all .qmd files (even if they are not in chapters). The additional explicit line for an older version (e.g. assignment-api-...-v001.qmd) is typically for “keep the old version” / “ensure it still renders” behavior: it forces the old version to be rendered even if later you switched chapters: or removed it from the main book structure.

title: 6  SPEC-initial-yrPublish-design-0.0.1-v001.qmd
text:
6.1 Purpose
Design yrPublish v0.0.1 as a thin bash CLI wrapper around Quarto that recreates the behavior of the pre-yrPublish toolset (yrPublish v0.0.0).
This version focuses on preserving existing custom behavior (custom chapter numbering via yrChapterNumber, renumbering via yrRenumberChapters, and the runtime DOM renumbering via yrstuff-v001.js).
This design is grounded in the pre-yrPublish working Quarto project found at sampleBooks/DataManagementUsingRSrc/DataManagementUsingRSrc-v0.0.0/, which contains the v0.0.0 scripts/assets we vendor unchanged into new yrPublish projects for v0.0.1.

title: 6  SPEC-initial-yrPublish-design-0.0.1-v001.qmd
text:
6.2 Scope
In scope:

A single bash CLI entrypoint named yrPublish with a small set of subcommands.
A project scaffolding workflow: yrPublish create-project ...
Render/preview workflows that pin and use the correct Quarto and yrPublish tooling.
Chapter-number workflows:

renumber sequentially in _quarto.yml
apply chapter numbers into .qmd files
ensure chapter-number display is corrected at runtime in the generated HTML

Version pinning:

record which yrPublish version the target project expects
vendor/copy the matching yrPublish tooling into the project repo

A version-reporting interface:

show the running yrPublish executable version
show the target project’s locked yrPublish version
warn on mismatch

Documentation of example/test strategy (describe-only in this spec; no scaffolding yet).

Out of scope:

Rewriting the existing v0.0.0 scripts/assets in-place.
Implementing v0.0.2 enhancements (these may change how scripts are managed).

title: 6  SPEC-initial-yrPublish-design-0.0.1-v001.qmd
text:
6.3 Functional Requirements

6.3.1 Non-negotiable checklist (v0.0.1)
These items must all be implemented for yrPublish v0.0.1 to be considered complete:

Provide a single bash CLI named yrPublish with the required subcommands (including help / help <subcommand>).
Implement version pinning and repo-local locking via .yrPublish/yrPublish.lock.
During yrPublish create-project, vendor/copy the pinned yrPublish implementation assets into .yrPublish/<locked-version>/....
Implement dependency isolation by pinning Quarto in .yrPublish/config/quarto-version.txt and using a vendored Quarto install under .yrPublish/tools/quarto/<version>/bin/quarto.
Implement yrPublish version-cli (baked into the running executable/script) and yrPublish version-project <project-path> (read from the project’s .yrPublish/yrPublish.lock).
Warn whenever the running yrPublish tool version and the project-locked yrPublish version differ (for any subcommand that operates on a project).
Implement chapter-number workflows:

yrPublish renumber-chapters updates # yrChapterNumber <n> comments in _quarto.yml sequentially.
yrPublish apply-chapter-numbers updates <yrChapterNumber>...</yrChapterNumber> in .qmd files.
yrPublish render automatically runs apply-chapter-numbers before invoking Quarto.

Treat the v0.0.0 scripts/assets as unchanged resources: do not edit them in-place for v0.0.1; only vendor/copy them.



6.3.2 Command surface
yrPublish is a bash CLI with subcommands (and help for each subcommand).
Required subcommands/options:

yrPublish create-project <project-name> [--type book|website|...]
yrPublish render [<project-path>]
yrPublish preview [<project-path>]
yrPublish renumber-chapters [<project-path>]
yrPublish apply-chapter-numbers [<project-path>]
yrPublish help
yrPublish help <subcommand>

Version/reporting subcommands (new in v0.0.1):

yrPublish version-cli

Reports the version baked into the running yrPublish executable/script.

yrPublish version-project [<project-path>]

Reports the yrPublish version required by the target project by reading .yrPublish/yrPublish.lock.


Version consistency behavior:

yrPublish version-cli always works without probing the target repo.
yrPublish version-project fails clearly if the target project has no .yrPublish/yrPublish.lock.
Any yrPublish subcommand that operates on a project should warn if:

version-cli (running tool version) differs from
version-project (locked project-required version).




6.3.3 Pinned assets and v0.0.0 reuse (unchanged resources)
All supporting tools/assets used to implement yrPublish v0.0.1 are to be placed in a .yrPublish/ folder at the root of each project created by yrPublish.
For v0.0.1, the v0.0.0 code is reused unchanged by vendoring it into the project for the locked version.
The v0.0.0 scripts/assets to vendor (copied unchanged) include:

yrChapterNumber-v004.sh
yrRenumberChapters-v005.sh
yrBuildQuarto-v004.sh
copy_quartoYml_to_bookFolder-v001.R
yrZipQmdFiles.R
yrstuff-v001.js
yrStyles-main-v001.css
yrNocacheHeaders-v001.html



6.3.4 Render pipeline behavior
yrPublish render must apply chapter numbers before calling Quarto, and the generated site must run runtime renumbering at view time.
Proposed pipeline:

yrPublish apply-chapter-numbers (so .qmd files contain updated <yrChapterNumber>...</yrChapterNumber> content)
Call the pinned local Quarto binary to perform the render
Rely on the project’s _quarto.yml hooks:

pre-render to ensure _quarto.yml is copied into _book/ (so the runtime JS can fetch it)
post-render to create .qmd.zip artifacts in _book/

Runtime JS (yrstuff-v001.js) overwrites displayed chapter/section numbering using the mapping found in _book/_quarto.yml.

Mermaid overview:
flowchart LR
  User[User runs yrPublish render] --> Apply[apply-chapter-numbers]
  Apply --> Render[quarto render]
  Render --> PreRender[pre-render: copy_quartoYml_to_bookFolder]
  PreRender --> PostRender[post-render: yrZipQmdFiles]
  Render --> Browser[client JS: yrstuff-v001 renumbers display]


6.3.5 Applying chapter numbers
yrPublish apply-chapter-numbers must:

Parse _quarto.yml chapter mappings that use # yrChapterNumber <n> comments.
Update the corresponding .qmd files’ <yrChapterNumber>...</yrChapterNumber> elements.

Help text requirement:

The help for apply-chapter-numbers must clearly state that this step is also run automatically during render.

title: 6  SPEC-initial-yrPublish-design-0.0.1-v001.qmd
text:
6.4 Version pinning and repo-local locking

6.4.1 Lockfile
Each yrPublish project repo must record which yrPublish version it expects in:

.yrPublish/yrPublish.lock

This file contains the locked yrPublish version (exact string).


6.4.2 Vendoring implementation assets
During yrPublish create-project:

yrPublish copies/vends the yrPublish implementation assets for the locked version into:

.yrPublish/<locked-version>/...

subsequent render / preview / etc uses the pinned assets from .yrPublish/<locked-version>/... rather than whatever scripts happen to exist globally.

This design must remain robust even if the developer upgrades the global yrPublish executable, because the project always uses the pinned toolset that matches its locked version.

title: 6  SPEC-initial-yrPublish-design-0.0.1-v001.qmd
text:
6.5 Dependency isolation mechanism
Document and implement one concrete approach:

Pin a Quarto version in .yrPublish/config/quarto-version.txt (written/templated by create-project).
yrPublish uses a local vendored Quarto install under .yrPublish/tools/quarto/<version>/bin/quarto.
A wrapper/shim ensures yrPublish subcommands call the vendored binary (not a random quarto from PATH).

title: 6  SPEC-initial-yrPublish-design-0.0.1-v001.qmd
text:
6.6 Proposed file/project layout (per created project)
repo-root/
  _quarto.yml                      # Quarto book config for the product
  ...content files...

  .yrPublish/
    yrPublish.lock                # locked yrPublish version expected by this repo
    config/
      quarto-version.txt
    tools/
      quarto/<version>/bin/quarto
    <locked-version>/
      yrChapterNumber-v004.sh
      yrRenumberChapters-v005.sh
      yrBuildQuarto-v004.sh
      copy_quartoYml_to_bookFolder-v001.R
      yrZipQmdFiles.R
      yrstuff-v001.js
      yrStyles-main-v001.css
      yrNocacheHeaders-v001.html

title: 6  SPEC-initial-yrPublish-design-0.0.1-v001.qmd
text:
6.7 Examples and tests (describe-only for v0.0.1)

6.7.1 Example project(s)
Create one or more example projects under examples/ that exercise all features, including:

renumber-chapters + apply-chapter-numbers
render output demonstrating runtime chapter numbering correction
verification that _book/_quarto.yml exists and that .qmd.zip artifacts are produced (if applicable to the existing wiring)

Each example must include a README.md describing how to run it.


6.7.2 Automated test strategy
Recommend (and document) a mix of:

CLI unit tests:

help text correctness
version subcommand behavior:

version-cli returns the baked executable version
version-project reads .yrPublish/yrPublish.lock
mismatch warning occurs when tool version differs from project lock


File-mutation tests:

apply-chapter-numbers updates <yrChapterNumber> in targeted .qmd files
renumber-chapters updates # yrChapterNumber comments in _quarto.yml

Integration tests (optional for v0.0.1, but documented):

run render in a temp directory with vendored assets
assert expected artifacts exist in _book/

title: 6  SPEC-initial-yrPublish-design-0.0.1-v001.qmd
text:
6.8 Acceptance Criteria

yrPublish subcommands exist and provide help output for each.
yrPublish render applies chapter-number renumbering and produces a rendered site where chapter numbers are corrected at runtime.
yrPublish create-project creates .yrPublish/ with:

.yrPublish/yrPublish.lock
pinned/vendored implementation assets under .yrPublish/<locked-version>/
pinned Quarto under .yrPublish/tools/quarto/...

yrPublish version-cli and yrPublish version-project both work and mismatch is warned.

title: 6  SPEC-initial-yrPublish-design-0.0.1-v001.qmd
text:
6.9 Risks and mitigations

Risk: project depends on runtime fetching of _book/_quarto.yml via JS.

Mitigation: pre-render must ensure _quarto.yml is copied into _book/ before the browser loads runtime JS.

Risk: global tool upgrades break old projects.

Mitigation: use .yrPublish/yrPublish.lock and vendor/copy matching yrPublish assets into .yrPublish/<locked-version>/.

title: 7  00010-initialDesignRequest
text:
7.1 Background
I have been creating several Quarto “book” projects that I render to HTML. Most of these are to support the classes that I teach. Some of these “books” have gotten quite large. I add material pretty regularly and these books gorw in size dynamically as time goes on. I keep the source code checked into git/github. For most of these books I have a 2nd github repo to which I copy the rendered website and serve that site through github pages.
For larger books, I use the github “part” concept to segment the book for easier navigation. For example these are some books that I created: https://y-rosenthal.github.io/DataManagementUsingR/ https://y-rosenthal.github.io/excelNotes/ https://y-rosenthal.github.io/rnotesLatest/ (see screen shots)
I’m also attaching the _quarto.yml file for those books.

title: 7  00010-initialDesignRequest
text:
7.1.1 -v00n version numbers
On many of my files, I include a “-v00N” version number that I increment when I create a new version of the file. I started using this system before I started to use git. However, even though I started to use git I find that it is still helpful sometimes to have a version number directly in the name of a file. In that way, if I use the same file in different books, I can very easily see which version of the file I’m using. For example, I am currently up to v004 for the yrChapterNumber Bash script. Therefore, the actual name of the script that I would run would be “yrChapterNumber-v004.sh”. For Bash scripts and utilities, I generally only increment the -v00N number when I make breaking changes. So I might checking numerous non-breaking changes to yrChapterNumber-v004 (e.g. changing the comments).
For .qmd files I have a bunch of -v00N files from before I started using git. Now that I am using git, I just kept the same filenames. Sometimes, I might create a new file with a -v00N extension even though I’m using git. The motivation is often that I want more than one versin of a file to be immediately available in the repo for convenience purposes.
This may not be the best system but it works for me now. It’s a system with very loose rules. I just want you to know what the -v00N endings on many files are in my repos.

title: 7  00010-initialDesignRequest
section: 7.2 Custom chapter numbers (enabled via css and javascript at runtime)
text:
7.2 Custom chapter numbers (enabled via css and javascript at runtime)

7.2.1 motivation
I often modify the books in the middle of my teaching a course. Sometimes, I might add a chapter in the middle of the book which by default would cause the later chapter numbers to be incremented. I didn’t like that since I didn’t want to change the chapter numbers in the book in the middle of a course after students had already become familiar with the book’s layout. My thought was to label a new chapter that appears after chapter 12 for example, not as chapter 13, but rather as chapter 12b. In that way I could keep all existing chapter numbers. Unfortunately, Quarto doesn’t seem to have a standard way to have custom chapter names. Therefore I devised my own solution using custom markup and Javascript.


7.2.2 custom markup
I added a  element to every first level heading in every page in the book. I then also added custom comments to the _quarto.yml at the end of every chapter line in the yml file that shows the preferred chapter number. For example, the following might be in a _quarto.yml. It shows the chapter numbers I want after # yrChapterNumber. In the following example (there is no actual book with the contents of this example), I might have had a book with 5 chapters (that didn’t have the emptyTags.qmd chapter). Suppose, I would want to add the emptyTags.qmd chapter in the middle of the semester. I would want the chapter number to be 1b and keep the rest of the chapter numbers the same.
- part: Intro to HTML
  chapters:
  - introToElements.qmd      # yrChapterNumber 1
  - emptyTags.qmd            # yrChapterNumber 1b
  - pageStructure.qmd        # yrChapterNumber 2
  - tableTags.qmd           # yrChapterNumber  3

- part: Intro to CSS
  chapters:
  - styleTag.qmd            # yrChapterNumber  4
  - basicSelectors.qmd      # yrChapterNumber  5
To support this, all .qmd files would have single top level # header at the top of the file. The text of the # header in a .qmd file becomes the text displayed in the table of contents (TOC) on the left of the Quarto book website. To support custom chapter numbers I added a  element in the single top level # heading in all .qmd files in the Quarto book. I also created a Bash script, yrChapterNumber.sh, that gets the preferred chapter numbers from the _quarto.yml and populates the  elements with the correct chapter numbers. I would run this bash script prior to rendering the book. So for exmple after running the yrChapterNumber.sh script I might have the following:
The first line of the “introToElements.qmd” file might ha: # 1.  Introduction to HTML element
The first line of the “emptyTags.qmd” might be: # 1b.  Empty tableTags
The first line of the pageStructure.qmd file might be: # 2.  Structure of a valid HTML page
etc.


7.2.3 Bash scripts
I have the following bash scripts:

yrChapterNumber.sh - described above in the previous section
yrRenumberChapter.sh :
Suppose for the next version of the book I want to start again with sequential chapter numbers. I would run the command yrRenumberChapters _quarto.yml. The script with change the numbers in the #yrChapterNumber comments in the _quarto.yml to be sequestial numbers, e.g. 1,2,3,4,5,6 instead of 1,1b,2,3,4,5 that was there before.
yrBuildQuarto.sh
I have a custom build script to support any other custom processing that I need to do to render the Quarto. For example at some point I think I needed to have the _quarto.yml file in the _book folder that contains the rendered website. The _quarto.yml is processed at runtime by custom javascript (in file yrstuff-v001.js)

I believe that




7.2.4 suppress original chapter numbers with custom css
The original Quarto rendered chapter numbers are suppressed from being displayed with the custom css in the file yrStyles-main-v0001.css, which currently contains the following:
.chapter-number {
    display: none;
}

yrchapternumber { font-size: 99%; color: red; }

.yrAppendixChapterNumber {
    display: none;
}


7.2.5 Custom javascript
I have custom javascript that renumbers the chapters from the numbers that Quarto renders into the book, into the the numbers that are in the  elements. (Note that appendices are treated a little differently due to how quarto renders appendix numbers and names).
I’ll tell you the truth - I don’t remember exactly how the following comes into play in the custom chapter numbering system system. However, it all works and I’m afraid to change it. Perhaps you could help me remember and understand how I designed this or perhaps you could point out some things that could be improved?
The javascript code in question in the file yrstuff-v001.js I believe it is part of this system. It parses the _quarto.yml file to map the new chapter numbers to the names of the qmd/html files. For this reason the _quarto.yml file must be in the rendered website’s folder (i.e. it must be copied to the _book folder at render time). This is accomplished by the R program, copy_quartoYml_to_bookFolder-v001.R program that is referenced in the pre-render: section in _quarto.yml. Note that the _quarto.yml file is also in the resources: section of the _quarto.yml file - I’m not sure if that is truly necessary or not - I don’t recall.
In the post-render: section of the _quarto.yml, I am also running an R script named yrZipQmdFiles.R that creates a .zip file from each .qmd file and puts it in the _book directory. I’m not 100% sure why I did that. If you can figure it out, let me know … I think that I might have wanted to add a link to each rendered HTML page that allows for the download of the source for the page. However, I don’t think I actually implemented that part - again I’m not sure - help me figure this out please …

title: 7  00010-initialDesignRequest
section: 7.3 Request for LLM to help me with
text:
7.3 Request for LLM to help me with
I need help with the following:

7.3.1 Request 1. Please help me try to answer the following questions
I have a few questions. Maybe you can help me figure this out. The following are the first few lines of my _quarto.yml file
project:
  type: book
  output-dir: _book
  pre-render: copy_quartoYml_to_bookFolder-v001.R
  post-render: Rscript yrZipQmdFiles.R
  resources:
    - _quarto.yml
    - yrNocacheHeaders-v001.html
    - yrstuff-v001.js
    - assignment-api-0000100-weatherDataApi-v002.qmd.zip
    - assignment-api-0000100-weatherDataApi-v001.qmd.zip
  render:
    - "*.qmd"  # Ensures all .qmd files are rendered, even if not in `chapters`
    - "assignment-api-0000100-weatherDataApi-v001.qmd"  # Ensure the old version gets rendered
QUESTIONS:

why did I use the Rscript command only in the post-render section but not in the pre-render section?
Is there any reference to or use of the .zip files that I create in the post-render step in the finished rendered website?
Why do I include _quarto.yml in the resources: section and also copy the _quarto.yml file to the _book folder in the pre-render: section
I’m not sure why I included the following. I think it was something I did when I was in a rush to get something working. If you see any particular reason why this is necessary, let me know. I don’t think this is necessary for the general approach to building these types of books.
render:
  - "*.qmd"  # Ensures all .qmd files are rendered, even if not in `chapters`
  - "assignment-api-0000100-weatherDataApi-v001.qmd"  # Ensure the old version gets rendered

title: 7  00010-initialDesignRequest
text:
7.4 Request 2. Document system
Please

title: 8  Create design spec
text:
8.1 include necessary dependencies
Look at @../../docs/BackgroundAndFeatures and answer the questions in the document. Please update the document with all of your answers in a way that clearly labels that the answers are coming from Cursor’s AI.
Please create a design spec for the v0.0.1 version of this system. Write the document in Quarto markdow format into the file, docs/SPEC-initial-yrPublish-design-0.0.1-v001.qmd (see @docs/DOC-GUIDE-v001.qmd for info about writing documentation). Make sure to add this file into the @docs quarto book and render the docs book.
Please design a system that allows me to run a single bash cli command called yrPublish that is a Quarto wrapper.
This initial version of yrPublish is simply meant to recreate the same functionality as was available in the pre-yrPublish set of tools and techniques (AKA yrPublish version 0.0.0) that I had used prior to creating yrPublish. Use the scripts and assets from the v0.0.0 book that is located in the following folder as an example and as a source of scripts and resources. sampleBooks/DataManagementUsingRSrc/DataManagementUsingRSrc-v0.0.0, For this initial version of yrPublish, do not modify the code in the script files that were used in the DataManagementUsingRSrc-v0.0.0 book. Rather incorporate them unchanged as resources that get invoked by yrPublish. Note that in v0.0.2 (next version) of yrPublish. I will be fine with modify that code. However, to make sure that we don’t make mistakes, I’d like v0.0.1 of yrPublish to not change that code directly.
The yrPublsih cli should have options (or sub commands) to
All supporting tools and assets that are used to implement the yrPublish functionality should be placed in a .yrPublish folder at the root of the project. For example the yrChapterNumber-v004.sh , @yrRenumberChapters-v005.sh and yrBuildQuarto-v004.sh Bash scripts should be somewhere under this folder.
Design a mechanism such that the repo has everything it needs installed into the repo. For example, install the verion of Quarto that is being used in the repo directly into the repo folder. Similarly, create some mechanism to ensure that the correct version of Quarto and similar assets are being called when using the yrPublish repo. I’m not sure exactly how to do this yet, but something in a similar vein to renv or similar tools that maintain isolation environments.

title: 8  00020-buildInitialSystem-answerQuestions
text:
8.1.1 Why did I use Rscript in post-render but not in pre-render?
Cursor AI answer: Quarto’s project pre-render / post-render scripts are supported for multiple languages (including R) via Quarto’s project-script mechanism. So the “correctness” generally does not depend on whether the entry is written as bare somefile.R or as an explicit Rscript somefile.R invocation. It’s most likely historical/style/convenience: pre-render: copy_quartoYml_to_bookFolder-v001.R is already valid, and post-render: Rscript yrZipQmdFiles.R is effectively just making the interpreter explicit.


8.1.2 Is there any reference to or use of the .zip files created in post-render?
Cursor AI answer: In your described setup, the .zip files are created into _book and are listed under resources: in _quarto.yml, which means they are intended to be published as static assets. However, that does not automatically mean the existing site UI/JS references them. In most implementations of this pattern, the zips are used for “downloadable source” (or archival) even if you haven’t yet added a link/button per page. To confirm actual usage, you’d look for references to those zip filenames in templates/HTML/JS; based on the repo structure here, the published assets exist, but the linkage may be unimplemented.


8.1.3 Why include _quarto.yml in resources: and also copy _quarto.yml to _book in pre-render?
Cursor AI answer: resources: is a Quarto mechanism for shipping static files into the output directory, so including _quarto.yml there ensures it becomes available in the built output. Separately, your pre-render step copies _quarto.yml into _book so runtime client-side JS (like yrstuff-v001.js) can fetch _quarto.yml from the final site. Keeping both is likely redundant: one mechanism may have been added to guarantee a working behavior during experimentation, or to ensure timing/paths matched what the JS fetch expects. Either way, the functional goal is the same: make _quarto.yml reliably available under _book/ at runtime.


8.1.4 Why include render: - "*.qmd" and also explicitly list one older .qmd?
Cursor AI answer: For type: book, the chapters: list controls what Quarto includes in navigation, but Quarto may still skip rendering some files depending on project configuration and incremental rendering. render: "*.qmd" is a way to force rendering of all .qmd files (even if they are not in chapters). The additional explicit line for an older version (e.g. assignment-api-...-v001.qmd) is typically for “keep the old version” / “ensure it still renders” behavior: it forces the old version to be rendered even if later you switched chapters: or removed it from the main book structure.

title: 8  Cursor AI answers (from Cursor’s AI)
text:
8.1 Create design spec
Please create a design spec for the v0.0.1 version of this system. Write the document in Quarto markdow format into the file, docs/SPEC-initial-yrPublish-design-0.0.1-v001.qmd (see @docs/DOC-GUIDE-v001.qmd for info about writing documentation). Make sure to add this file into the @docs quarto book and render the docs book.
Please design a system that allows me to run a single bash cli command called yrPublish that is a Quarto wrapper.
This initial version of yrPublish is simply meant to recreate the same functionality as was available in the pre-yrPublish set of tools and techniques (AKA yrPublish version 0.0.0) that I had used prior to creating yrPublish. Use the scripts and assets from the v0.0.0 book that is located in the following folder as an example and as a source of scripts and resources. sampleBooks/DataManagementUsingRSrc/DataManagementUsingRSrc-v0.0.0, For this initial version of yrPublish, do not modify the code in the script files that were used in the DataManagementUsingRSrc-v0.0.0 book. Rather incorporate them unchanged as resources that get invoked by yrPublish. Note that in v0.0.2 (next version) of yrPublish. I will be fine with modify that code. However, to make sure that we don’t make mistakes, I’d like v0.0.1 of yrPublish to not change that code directly.
The yrPublsih cli should have options (or sub commands) to

create an initial repo for a yrPublish rendered product (e.g. book, presentation, etc)
render the repo (similar to quarto render) (see @yrBuildQuarto-v004.sh)
preview the repo (similar to quarto preview) (see @yrBuildQuarto-v004.sh)
renumber the chapters sequentially and store the new numbers in the _quarto.yml file (as in @yrRenumberChapters-v005.sh)
apply the chapter numbers from the _quarto.yml to the .qmd files (as in @yrChapterNumber-v004.sh). Note that this should automatically happen when the site it rendered, but this should also be an option to run separately when someone wants to. The help for this command should clearly state upfront that the command is also run when a render is done.
display help about the command. There should also be a help option for all sub commands.

All supporting tools and assets that are used to implement the yrPublish functionality should be placed in a .yrPublish folder at the root of the project. For example the yrChapterNumber-v004.sh , @yrRenumberChapters-v005.sh and yrBuildQuarto-v004.sh Bash scripts should be somewhere under this folder.
crumbs: 8  Cursor AI answers (from Cursor's AI)

title: 8  Cursor AI answers (from Cursor’s AI)
text:
8.2 include necessary dependencies
Design a mechanism such that the repo has everything it needs installed into the repo. For example, install the verion of Quarto that is being used in the repo directly into the repo folder. Similarly, create some mechanism to ensure that the correct version of Quarto and similar assets are being called when using the yrPublish repo. I’m not sure exactly how to do this yet, but something in a similar vein to renv or similar tools that maintain isolation environments.
crumbs: 8  Cursor AI answers (from Cursor's AI)

title: 8  Create design spec
text:
8.2 Create example project(s)
Create one or more example projects that exercise all features of yrPublish. Put these in the examples folder. Also create a README.md in the @examples folder that explains what is in that folder. Each example project should be in a separate subfolder and include a README.md file that explains everything about that example.

title: 8  Create design spec
text:
8.3 Create test cases
Create a collection of automated tests for the system that test all features of the system. I’m not sure if these test cases should be directly tied to the example projects or not, please analyze this issue and make a recommendation along with the design of how to implement it.

title: 8  Create design spec
text:
8.4 Please ask …
Please grill me on questions you have …

title: 9  00030-implementSPEC-0.0.1
text:
9.1 Cursor AI generated prompt (for your implementation chat)
This prompt was created by Cursor AI in response to your request to generate a starter prompt.
You are implementing yrPublish v0.0.1 according to the design spec in:

title: 9  00030-implementSPEC-0.0.1
text:
../../SPEC-initial-yrPublish-design-0.0.1-v001.qmd


9.1.1 Goal
Build a single bash CLI named yrPublish that wraps Quarto and provides the v0.0.1 subcommands described in the spec.


9.1.2 Key highlights (do not miss these)
The bullets below are the most commonly missed details, but you must implement the entire v0.0.1 SPEC document. Do not stop at these bullets—review and implement every requirement/acceptance criterion in: - ../../SPEC-initial-yrPublish-design-0.0.1-v001.qmd

Version pinning + repo-local locking

Each yrPublish project must have .yrPublish/yrPublish.lock.
During yrPublish create-project, yrPublish must vendor/copy the yrPublish implementation assets for the locked version into:

.yrPublish/<locked-version>/...

All subsequent commands must use the pinned vendored assets.

Dependency isolation

Pin a Quarto version in .yrPublish/config/quarto-version.txt.
Use a vendored Quarto install under .yrPublish/tools/quarto/<version>/bin/quarto.
Ensure all subcommands run the vendored Quarto binary, not the global quarto from PATH.

Version reporting

Implement:

yrPublish version-cli → report the version baked into the running executable/script (do not read the project).
yrPublish version-project <project-path> → read required version from .yrPublish/yrPublish.lock.

Any project-operating subcommand should warn if version-cli != version-project.

Chapter numbering behavior

Implement:

yrPublish renumber-chapters to sequentially renumber # yrChapterNumber <n> comments in _quarto.yml using the existing v0.0.0 script logic (vendored unchanged for v0.0.1).
yrPublish apply-chapter-numbers to update <yrChapterNumber>...</yrChapterNumber> inside the .qmd files using the existing v0.0.0 script logic (vendored unchanged for v0.0.1).

yrPublish render must automatically run apply-chapter-numbers before invoking Quarto.




9.1.3 Constraints

For v0.0.1, do not modify the v0.0.0 scripts/assets directly; treat them as unchanged resources that are copied/vendored into projects.



9.1.4 What to produce in this implementation chat

Code changes to add the yrPublish CLI (including help + help <subcommand>).
The create-project workflow that scaffolds .yrPublish/ with:

yrPublish.lock
vendored/pinned yrPublish implementation assets under .yrPublish/<locked-version>/
vendored Quarto under .yrPublish/tools/quarto/...

Any internal helper scripts needed under the repo’s .yrPublish/ design area (as required by the spec).
Update or add tests and/or example project scaffolding as described in the spec.



9.1.5 Verification steps (explain how you verified)

How to test yrPublish version-cli and yrPublish version-project.
How to test that create-project produces the expected .yrPublish/ structure and pinned assets.
How to test render ensures chapter numbers are applied (and how to verify runtime JS renumbering works).

If anything is ambiguous or under-specified, ask clarifying questions before coding.
